title: About
text: Hi! My name is Katie Freeman, and I’m a junior at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln double majoring in advertising and public relations and sports media communication with a minor in english. I’ve been on the dean’s list every semester in college. Currently, I am a Runner/Agency Assistant at Swanson Russell. I assist with administrative tasks and day-to-day office operations. Previously, I was the marketing and special events intern at the Brain Injury Association of Nebraska, where I created advertising campaigns on Canva and Adobe Creative Cloud, managed and shared on social media accounts including Facebook and Instagram, and helped with the planning and execution of their annual conference. I also worked closely with the marketing coordinator to create advertising strategies to help spread brain injury awareness. I am a go-getter, self-starter, leader of group projects and a team player. This is my sports blog for SPMC 350: Sport Data Analysis and Visualization.

title: Huskers vs Hawkeyes: Who Was Really Better in the 2025-2026 Basketball Season?
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(patchwork)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggalt)


Comparing the rivalry between Nebraska and Iowa in the 2025-2026 basketball season.
On paper, Nebraska appeared to have the stronger resume. The Huskers ended the season 28-7, while Iowa finished 24-13. Nebraska also held the better record and a higher national ranking.
Although, records don’t always tell the full story. To understand how these teams actually performed, we need to go beyond wins and losses and examine how they compare across key metrics within the conference. So let’s take a closer look at some data compared to other Big Ten teams and see where Nebraska and Iowa really stand and who was truly better.


Code
bigten <- read_csv("data/bigtenstats2526.csv")

nu <- bigten |>
  filter(School == "Nebraska")

ia <- bigten |>
  filter(School == "Iowa")

ggplot() +
  geom_point(data=bigten, aes(x=PTS, y=Opp_PTS)) +
  geom_smooth(data=bigten, aes(x=PTS, y=Opp_PTS), method="lm") +
  geom_text_repel(data=bigten, aes(x=PTS, y=Opp_PTS, label=School)) +
  geom_encircle(data=nu, aes(x=PTS, y=Opp_PTS), s_shape=.01, expand=.01, color="#e41c38") +
  geom_encircle(data=ia, aes(x=PTS, y=Opp_PTS), s_shape=.01, expand=.01, color="#FFCD00") +
  labs(
    x="Points Per Game", 
    y="Opponents Points Per Game", 
    title="Nearly identical in scoring", 
    subtitle="Nebraska holds a slight edge, but both teams perform statistically close in points per game.", 
    caption="Source: SportsDataVerse | By: Katie Freeman"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 









Code
ggsave("image.png")


Nebraska and Iowa fall outside the typical range of Big Ten teams in points per game versus opponent points per game, but more importantly, they sit right next to each other.
Both teams averaged around 75 points per game while holding their opponents under 70 points. Nebraska had a slight edge in scoring margin, but overall, the two were far more similar than their records suggest.
To dig deeper, we can look at key categories like field goal percentage, 3-point percentage, rebounds and free throw percentage to compare the two teams from this season.


Code
bar1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=bigten, aes(x=reorder(School, `FG%`), weight=`FG%`), fill="lightgrey") +
  geom_bar(data=nu, aes(x=reorder(School, `FG%`), weight=`FG%`), fill="#e41c38") +
  geom_bar(data=ia, aes(x=reorder(School, `FG%`), weight=`FG%`), fill="#FFCD00") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="",
    y="Field Goal Percentage"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
           
bar2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=bigten, aes(x=reorder(School, `3P%`), weight=`3P%`), fill="lightgrey") +
  geom_bar(data=nu, aes(x=reorder(School, `3P%`), weight=`3P%`), fill="#e41c38") +
  geom_bar(data=ia, aes(x=reorder(School, `3P%`), weight=`3P%`), fill="#FFCD00") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="",
    y="3-Point Percentage"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
  
bar3 <- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=bigten, aes(x=reorder(School, TRB), weight=TRB), fill="lightgrey") +
  geom_bar(data=nu, aes(x=reorder(School, TRB), weight=TRB), fill="#e41c38") +
  geom_bar(data=ia, aes(x=reorder(School, TRB), weight=TRB), fill="#FFCD00") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="",
    y="Rebounds"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
  
bar4 <- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=bigten, aes(x=reorder(School, `FT%`), weight=`FT%`), fill="lightgrey") +
  geom_bar(data=nu, aes(x=reorder(School, `FT%`), weight=`FT%`), fill="#e41c38") +
  geom_bar(data=ia, aes(x=reorder(School, `FT%`), weight=`FT%`), fill="#FFCD00") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="",
    y="Free Throw Percentage"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

bar1 + bar2 + bar3 + bar4 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Where Nebraska and Iowa separate in key metrics",
    subtitle = "Field goal %, three-point %, rebounds and free throw % across the Big Ten.",
    caption = "Source: Sports Reference | By: Katie Freeman"
  ) & 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=12), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )









Looking at shooting efficiency, Iowa separates itself. The Hawkeyes rank near the top in field goal percentage, outperforming the Huskers, indicating they are slightly more efficient in scoring.
From beyond the arc, the two teams are nearly identical, both sitting in the middle of the Big Ten, with Iowa holding a slight advantage on 3-point percentage.
Rebounding tells a different story. Nebraska holds a clear advantage, while Iowa ranks second to the bottom of the conference. This suggests Nebraska created more second-chance opportunities.
At the free throw line, Iowa sits slightly higher than Nebraska. Iowa ranks near the top of the Big Ten, while Nebraska remains more average.
Since the two teams were so similar in their three-point percentage, let’s take a deeper look at the three-point shooting for both teams.


Code
ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data=bigten,
    aes(x=`3P`, y=`3P%`, size=`3PA`),
    color="lightgrey",
    alpha = .3) +
  geom_point(
    data=nu,
    aes(x=`3P`, y=`3P%`, size=`3PA`),
    color="#e41c38",
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data=ia, 
    aes(x=`3P`, y=`3P%`, size=`3PA`),
    color="#FFCD00",
  ) +
  scale_size(range = c(8,20), name="3 Point Attempts") +
    geom_text_repel(
      data=bigten,
      aes(x=`3P`, y=`3P%`, label=School)
    ) +
labs(
    x="3-Pointers Made", 
    y="3-Point Percentage", 
    title="Nebraska shoots more, Iowa shoots smarter", 
    subtitle="Iowa falls short in 3-pointers made, but stays close in 3-point percentage.", 
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | By: Katie Freeman"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=12), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot")









Nebraska is known for volume shooting and Iowa is more selective but efficient. Nebraska takes more threes, and as a result, they make more overall. Iowa, on the other hand, prioritizes efficiency over quantity.
Nebraska and Iowa played multiple times throughout this season, with each team securing a win. But the most important game came in the Sweet 16, where Iowa secured a 77-71 win in the biggest game the two ever played with each other.
That last one mattered the most. When the season was on the line, Iowa shot better, defended better and ended up winning.
So, who was the better team?
Nebraska had the stronger season by traditional measures such as record, standings and overall resume. They were consistent from start to finish.
But Iowa proved to be the more dangerous team. Their efficiency, defensive strength and ability to perform under pressure ultimately defined them.
These visualizations highlight what standings alone cannot. Ultimately, Nebraska had the better season, but Iowa was the more complete team when it mattered most.

title: No One Shoots More or Better Than Pryce Sandfort in the Big Ten
text:
Examining the Three-Point Efficiency of the Husker Forward Among Big Ten Players.
Players who take a high volume of shots from beyond the arc usually see their efficiency dip. As defenses focus in on them, attempts become more difficult and consistency becomes harder to maintain. In college basketball, especially in a competitive league like the Big Ten, players generally fall into one of two categories. High-volume shooters or highly efficient ones.
But Pryce Sandfort may just be both.
This raises an important question: Is Pryce Sandfort the most effective high-volume three-point shooter in the conference?
To answer this question, I analyzed the 2025-2026 Big Ten men’s basketball player statistics from Sports Reference. I defined high-volume shooters as players attempting at least five three-pointers per game and I measured efficiency by using a players three-point percentage.
By combining makes, attempts and accuracy, we can identify players who can maintain precision while taking a large number of shots.
Before evaluating efficiency, it’s important to understand just how much Sandfort is shooting. Volume can separate players and Sandfort stands out immediately in the conference.
Focusing on Big Ten players averaging at least five three-point attempts per game, Sandfort leads the conference in total attempts.


Code
library(tidyverse)

#| warning: false # Exclude any warnings from the chunk

bigten <- read_csv("data/bigten.csv")

nu <- bigten |>
  filter(Team == "Nebraska")

nu5 <- bigten |>
  filter(`3PA` >= 5, Team == "Nebraska")

shooters <- bigten |>
  filter(`3PA` >= 5)

ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=shooters, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3PA`), weight=`3PA`), fill="grey") +
  geom_bar(data=nu5, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3PA`), weight=`3PA`), fill="#e41c38") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="Player", 
    y="3-Point Attempts", 
    title="Sandfort Sits at the Top for Shooting Volume",
    subtitle="Among players averaging at least 5 threes per game, he leads the Big Ten in three-point attempts.", 
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | By: Katie Freeman"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 









Sandfort doesn’t just take a lot of threes, he takes more than anyone else in the Big Ten.
However, this often comes with a downside. The more a player shoots, the more difficult those shots become, leading to lower percentages.
So, the question becomes: Is Sandfort sacrificing efficiency for volume?
To answer this question, we need to compare Sandfort’s efficiency with other high-volume shooters.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggtext)

bigten <- read_csv("data/bigten.csv")

shooters <- bigten |>
  filter(`3PA` >= 5)

nu5 <- bigten |>
  filter(`3PA` >= 5, Team == "Nebraska")

ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data=bigten,
    aes(x=`3P`, y=`3P%`, size=`3PA`),
    color="grey",
    alpha = .3) +
  geom_point(
    data=nu5,
    aes(x=`3P`, y=`3P%`, size=`3PA`),
    color="#e41c38"
  ) +
  scale_size(range = c(1,10), name="3-Point Attempts") +
    geom_text_repel(
      data=bigten,
      aes(x=`3P`, y=`3P%`, label=Player)
    ) +
labs(
    x="3-Point Field Goals", 
    y="3-Point Percentage", 
    title="High Volume Doesn't Mean Lower Efficiency for Sandfort", 
    subtitle="Most high-volume shooters see their accuracy drop, but Sandfort proves that doesn't have to happen.", 
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | By: Katie Freeman"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot")









While many players trend toward lower efficiency as volume increases, Sandfort stands out. He hovers near 40% while maintaining one of the highest shot totals in the conference.
Combining elite volume, high total makes and strong accuracy, his position on the chart puts him in a rare category among Big Ten players.
This chart doesn’t just show how often he shoots, but how well he shoots, which few players are able to combine at this level.


Code
bar1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=shooters, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3P%`), weight=`3P%`), fill="grey") +
  geom_bar(data=nu5, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3P%`), weight=`3P%`), fill="#e41c38") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="",
    y="3-Point Percentage"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

bar2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=shooters, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3P`), weight=`3P`), fill="grey") +
  geom_bar(data=nu5, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3P`), weight=`3P`), fill="#e41c38") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="",
    y="3-Pointers Made"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() 

#| warning: false # Exclude any warnings from the chunk

library(patchwork)

bar2 + bar1 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Sandfort Makes More and Shoots Better Than Most",
    subtitle = "He leads the Big Ten in three-pointers made and sits among the conference's most accurate shooters.",
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | By: Katie Freeman"
  )  & 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )









Sandfort leads the conference in three-pointers made per game while also ranking near the top in three-point percentage. It’s impressive to be efficient at a high volume, it’s even more impressive to rank among the best in both categories.
This combination is what makes Sandfort a dangerous player.
Defenses can’t afford to leave him open because of his efficiency. At the same time, they can’t limit his impact, because his volume ensures he will continue to get opportunities.
This dual threat forces opponents into difficult decisions with every possession, and more often than not, Sandfort holds the advantage.
He isn’t just a high-volume shooter. He isn’t just an efficient one. He is both! That’s what makes him arguably the most effective high-volume three-point shooter in the Big Ten.
